metric_value: No commercially available client/server WMS existed in the US marketplace in mid-1994
notes: Core finding of the memo; based on systematic research of WMS market

metric_value: Two primary modes: RF handhelds and DEC VT 100/200 terminals in emulation mode — neither is client/server
notes: Suppliers often called handhelds clients but this did not meet Aberdeen's client/server definition

metric_value: Application cleaved such that user-facing portion runs locally and business logic runs on another computer
notes: Aberdeen's formal definition used to evaluate whether WMS products qualified as client/server

metric_value: Manhattan Associates PkMS was the first commercially released client/server WMS — shipped 1995
notes: Key finding establishing Manhattan Associates as the pioneer in client/server WMS

metric_value: Catalyst used Windows and Vermont Views on RF handhelds in 1993-94 but did not offer true client/server
notes: Catalyst was an early WMS provider but did not ship true client/server until GUI desktop in 1996

metric_value: Catalyst shipped GUI desktop program in 1996 — first true client/server replacement for VT 200s
notes: Two years after 1994 timeframe in question

metric_value: AS/400s were used in a host-dumb terminal environment in 1994 for warehouse management
notes: AS/400 was a common WMS platform but operated in host-terminal mode not client/server

metric_value: Manhattan Associates first installations were in 1991 on both Unix and the AS/400
notes: Early Manhattan Associates deployments were host-based not client/server

metric_value: JBA had a host-dumb terminal warehouse management system on the AS/400 in 1994
Computer Associates had BOSS warehouse management system on the AS/400 in 1994
Client/server technology was available in 1994 and before but no commercial WMS products used it
notes: Distinguishes between the existence of client/server architecture and its adoption in WMS products

metric_value: DEC VT 100/200 terminals in emulation mode were a standard warehouse communication method
notes: Part of the two dominant warehouse communication modes in 1994

metric_value: Manhattan Associates positioned as pioneer in client/server WMS with strong first-mover advantage
notes: Implicit prediction: being first to market with client/server WMS would yield competitive advantage

metric_value: Manhattan Associates is now a $6B+ market cap NASDAQ-listed supply chain solutions leader
notes: First-mover advantage in client/server WMS translated to long-term market leadership

metric_value: Catalyst International was acquired by RedPrairie (now Blue Yonder) — failed to maintain independent position
notes: Late entry into client/server WMS may have contributed to competitive disadvantage
